location.origin: Seattle, Washington / Los Angeles, California. The Postal Service was a collaboration between Ben Gibbard (Seattle) and Jimmy Tamborello (Los Angeles). The album was created by mailing tracks back and forth -- hence the name.
location.formative: The project emerged from Gibbard's Death Cab world (Seattle) and Tamborello's electronic production (L.A.). Give Up (2003) was one of the best-selling indie albums ever. Sub Pop Records.
location.career: The Postal Service: Ben Gibbard's THIRD appearance (Death Cab 58, solo 60, Postal Service 86). First artist to appear three times. One album (Give Up, 2003). Platinum. Indie Electronic -- FIRST electronic genre in the engine. Genre Stage 68. E=4. Fourth Sub Pop artist.

labels: Sub Pop Records (fourth Sub Pop artist in engine; Give Up went platinum; one of Sub Pop's most commercially successful releases)

members.name: Ben Gibbard
members.note: THIRD appearance in the engine: Death Cab (58), solo (60), Postal Service (86). First artist to appear three times. Three genres: Indie Rock, Singer-Songwriter, Indie Electronic.

members.name: Jimmy Tamborello
members.note: Also of Dntel. Electronic producer. Created the programmed beats and synth textures that define the Postal Service sound. The electronic half of the collaboration.

members.name: Jenny Lewis
members.note: Of Rilo Kiley. Contributed vocals on several Give Up tracks including 'Nothing Better.' Guest role, not permanent member.

associated_musicians.name: Death Cab for Cutie
associated_musicians.note: Indie Rock at E=4. The primary project. Same vocalist, different genre, different collaborators. Death Cab is the band; Postal Service is the side project.

associated_musicians.name: Ben Gibbard solo
associated_musicians.note: Singer-Songwriter at E=2. Three Gibbard contexts: band (58), solo (60), side project (86). E=4, E=2, E=4.

associated_musicians.name: Chong the Nomad
associated_musicians.note: Electronic/Hip-Hop. Nearest electronic precedent. But categorized under Hip-Hop. Postal Service is the first primary electronic genre.

associated_musicians.name: Chris Walla
associated_musicians.note: Death Cab guitarist/producer. Gibbard without Walla is a different sonic world. The Postal Service is what Gibbard sounds like with Tamborello instead of Walla.

associated_musicians.name: Sam Beam
associated_musicians.note: Both on Sub Pop. Beam's quiet folk (E=2) and Postal Service's electronic pulse (E=4) -- Sub Pop's range from whisper to synthesizer.

theory_nodes.concept: The electronic pulse (eighty-seventh acoustic element -- programmed electronic beats layered with indie vocals; synthesizer arpeggios, glitchy drum machines, filtered bass, voice floating over electronic texture; the pulse is constant, the drum machine never tires; the voice is the only organic element)
examples: The Postal Service (degree 86) -- Give Up (2003). Programmed beats, synth layers, Gibbard's voice. The electronic pulse.
'Such Great Heights' -- the defining example. Glitchy beats, soaring synths, Gibbard's voice. The pulse carries the song.
Contrast with the stripped confession (degree 85, Votolato) -- Votolato removed everything. Postal Service adds everything. Both center on the voice. Different contexts.
The electronic pulse is constant -- the drum machine never breathes, never varies. The human voice is the variable. Machine precision plus human imprecision.
theory_nodes.description: The electronic pulse is the eighty-seventh acoustic element. Programmed electronic beats layered with indie vocals. The pulse is constant. The voice is the only organic element.

ancestors: The stripped confession (degree 85, Votolato) -- maximum subtraction. The electronic pulse is maximum addition.
The confessional (degree 46, Votolato) -- human voice as center. The electronic pulse also centers the voice, but surrounds it with electronic texture.
genealogy.relationship: Eighty-seven acoustic elements across eighty-six degrees. The electronic pulse follows the stripped confession -- from subtraction to addition. Track element 88 at degree 87.

theory_nodes.concept: The side project (eighty-seventh artist-city pattern -- artist from existing band forms separate project with different collaborator, genre, identity; one album; reveals the artist's range when primary constraints are removed)
examples: The Postal Service (degree 86) -- Gibbard's side project. One album. One collaborator (Tamborello). One genre (Indie Electronic) that doesn't exist in Death Cab.
The side project reveals range: Gibbard goes from Indie Rock (Death Cab) to Indie Electronic (Postal Service). Same voice, entirely different sonic world.
Contrast with the confessional return (degree 85, Votolato) -- the return stays in genre. The side project leaves genre entirely.
theory_nodes.description: The side project is the eighty-seventh artist-city pattern. Artist from existing band forms separate project. One album. Maximum range.

ancestors: The confessional return (degree 85, Votolato) -- return within genre. The side project leaves genre.
The recursive return (degree 82, Death Cab) -- Gibbard's Death Cab returns. The Postal Service is Gibbard outside Death Cab entirely.
genealogy.relationship: Eighty-seven artist-city patterns across eighty-six degrees. Track pattern 88 at degree 87.

theory_nodes.concept: First electronic genre -- Indie Electronic is the FIRST electronic genre in the engine; after 85 degrees of acoustic/rock/folk/indie; Genre Stage 68; new sonic territory; synthesizers, drum machines, programmed beats
examples: The Postal Service (degree 86) -- Indie Electronic. FIRST electronic genre.
Chong the Nomad (degree 44) was electronic but categorized under Hip-Hop. Postal Service is the first PRIMARY electronic genre.
After 85 degrees of organic instruments, the engine encounters synthesizers and drum machines for the first time.
theory_nodes.description: First electronic genre at degree 86. Indie Electronic. Genre Stage 68. New sonic territory after 85 degrees of organic genres.

ancestors: Chong the Nomad (degree 44) -- electronic elements under Hip-Hop. Not primary.
All prior genres (1-85) -- acoustic, rock, folk, indie, alternative. No primary electronic.
genealogy.relationship: FIRST electronic genre at degree 86. Track future electronic genres.

theory_nodes.concept: Gibbard's third appearance -- first artist to appear THREE times; Death Cab (58, Indie Rock, E=4), solo (60, Singer-Songwriter, E=2), Postal Service (86, Indie Electronic, E=4); three genres, 28 degrees; E=4/E=2/E=4 pattern
examples: Ben Gibbard: Death Cab (58) + solo (60) + Postal Service (86). Three genres. First triple appearance.
Energy pattern: E=4, E=2, E=4. Full with band, quiet alone, full with collaborator.
Three genres in 28 degrees: Indie Rock, Singer-Songwriter, Indie Electronic. Maximum artistic range.
theory_nodes.description: Gibbard's third appearance at degree 86. First artist to appear three times. Three genres. E=4/E=2/E=4 pattern.

ancestors: Death Cab (degree 58) -- first Gibbard. Indie Rock.
Ben Gibbard solo (degree 60) -- second Gibbard. Singer-Songwriter.
genealogy.relationship: First triple appearance at degree 86. Track future multi-appearance artists.

theory_nodes.concept: E=4 at degree 86 -- full electronic energy; programmed beats fill the frequency spectrum; same energy as Death Cab (58); pattern: E=4/E=2/E=4; electronic energy is different from rock energy but same level
examples: The Postal Service (degree 86) -- E=4. Programmed beats, layered synths, full frequency spectrum.
Same E=4 as Death Cab (58). Different source: organic instruments (58) vs electronic production (86). Same energy level.
Gibbard's pattern: E=4 (band), E=2 (solo), E=4 (collaboration). Energy correlates with collaboration, not genre.
theory_nodes.description: E=4 at degree 86. Full electronic energy. Same level as Death Cab (58) but different source. Electronic energy vs rock energy.

ancestors: E=3 at degree 85 (Votolato). Rises to E=4.
E=4 at degree 58 (Death Cab). Gibbard returns to E=4.

theory_nodes.concept: Sub Pop Records -- fourth artist; Give Up went platinum; one of Sub Pop's most commercially successful releases; Sub Pop's range from grunge to indie electronic
examples: The Postal Service (degree 86) -- Sub Pop Records. Fourth artist.
Give Up (2003) went platinum. One of the best-selling indie albums ever.
Sub Pop's range: grunge, folk, indie electronic. The label contains multitudes.
theory_nodes.description: Sub Pop Records fourth artist at degree 86. Give Up went platinum. Sub Pop's range expands to electronic.

ancestors: Sam Beam (degree 68) on Sub Pop. Third artist.
Previous Sub Pop entries. The label's catalog grows.
genealogy.relationship: Fourth Sub Pop artist at degree 86. Track future appearances.

cross_references.relationship: Gibbard's band. Indie Rock at E=4. The primary project. Same vocalist, different genre, different collaborators, different sonic identity. Death Cab is the band; Postal Service is the side project.
Gibbard alone. Singer-Songwriter at E=2. Three Gibbard contexts: band (58), solo (60), side project (86). E=4, E=2, E=4. First artist to appear three times.
Electronic connection. Electronic/Hip-Hop. Nearest electronic precedent. But Chong was categorized under Hip-Hop. Postal Service is the first primary electronic genre.
Death Cab universe. Death Cab guitarist/producer. Gibbard without Walla is a different sonic world. The Postal Service is Gibbard with Tamborello instead of Walla.
Sub Pop. Both on Sub Pop Records. Beam's quiet folk (E=2) and Postal Service's electronic pulse (E=4) -- Sub Pop's range from whisper to synthesizer.
